text:
HEMATOLOGY/BONE MARROW TRANSPLANT PROGRAM
OUTPATIENT CLINIC NOTE - DAY +100 POST-ALLOGENEIC HEMATOPOIETIC STEM CELL TRANSPLANT

ATTENDING PHYSICIAN: [Attending BMT Hematologist]
FELLOW: [BMT Fellow]
NP: [Transplant Nurse Practitioner]

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT / REASON FOR VISIT:
Formalized 100-day post-allogeneic hematopoietic stem cell transplant evaluation. Ms. Margaret Okonkwo is a 48-year-old woman with AML with myelodysplasia-related changes (AML-MRC), ELN 2022 adverse risk, FLT3-ITD high allelic ratio, now day +100 following 10/10 matched unrelated donor peripheral blood stem cell transplant (MUD PBSCT) with reduced-intensity conditioning. She presents today for comprehensive 100-day evaluation.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
Ms. Okonkwo is well known to our BMT service. By way of brief summary: She is a 48-year-old Nigerian-American licensed practical nurse with a prior history of myelodysplastic syndrome (RCMD, diagnosed approximately 18 months before her current leukemia diagnosis) who presented with leukocytosis (WBC 84,000/uL), severe anemia, thrombocytopenia, and constitutional symptoms and was found to have AML with myelodysplasia-related changes per the 2022 WHO classification. Diagnostic bone marrow biopsy confirmed 62% CD34-positive myeloblasts. Cytogenetics showed a predominantly normal 46,XX karyotype with a minor del(7q) clone (residual MDS-related clone in 2/20 metaphases). Comprehensive NGS panel revealed a high allelic ratio FLT3-ITD (VAF 0.82), DNMT3A p.R882H, ASXL1 frameshift, SRSF2 p.P95H, and RUNX1 p.R204Q -- a mutational landscape consistent with AML arising from antecedent MDS and placing her firmly in the ELN adverse-risk category. Pre-treatment imaging showed mild splenomegaly without extramedullary disease; echocardiogram demonstrated a normal EF of 62%, clearing her for anthracycline-based therapy.

She received standard 7+3 induction chemotherapy (cytarabine 200 mg/m2 continuous infusion x7 days plus daunorubicin 60 mg/m2 days 1-3) with midostaurin 50 mg BID on days 8-21, per the RATIFY protocol. Induction was complicated by febrile neutropenia with E. coli bacteremia, Grade 3 mucositis requiring morphine PCA and temporary TPN, and significant transfusion requirements, all of which resolved. Day +30 bone marrow biopsy confirmed morphologic complete remission with MRD negativity by 8-color flow cytometry and FLT3-ITD undetectable by PCR -- an excellent and gratifying response to induction.

Given her adverse-risk disease profile, allogeneic stem cell transplant in CR1 was strongly pursued. While awaiting donor identification, she received one cycle of HiDAC consolidation (cytarabine 3 g/m2 q12h on days 1, 3, 5) plus midostaurin, which was complicated by Grade 2 cerebellar neurotoxicity after five of six planned doses of cytarabine; cytarabine was held and she recovered neurologically without lasting deficit. Pre-transplant bone marrow evaluation confirmed sustained MRD-negative CR1 with complete molecular clearance of FLT3-ITD and DNMT3A mutations by digital PCR. PET-CT imaging at that time showed no evidence of extramedullary or metabolically active disease.

She underwent 10/10 HLA-matched unrelated donor (male donor, CMV D-/R+ serostatus mismatch) peripheral blood stem cell transplant with a reduced-intensity conditioning regimen consisting of fludarabine/busulfan (Flu/Bu2 RIC), with rabbit ATG (Thymoglobulin) for additional T-cell depletion, and GVHD prophylaxis with tacrolimus plus short-course MTX. She received a CD34+ cell dose of 5.8 x 10^6/kg. Stem cells infused on day 0 without acute reaction.

Post-transplant course:
- Engraftment: Neutrophil engraftment confirmed on day +16 (ANC >500 for 3 consecutive days). Platelet engraftment on day +20. Day +30 bone marrow biopsy showed 98% donor chimerism (whole marrow), 99% myeloid chimerism, 95% T-cell chimerism, MRD negative, no evidence of relapse.
- CMV reactivation: Detected on day +45 (CMV PCR 1,840 IU/mL), asymptomatic, treated pre-emptively with valganciclovir 900 mg BID; CMV PCR became undetectable by day +60, valganciclovir stepped down to prophylactic dosing and continued through day +100.
- Chronic GVHD (day +90): Patient developed NIH global mild chronic GVHD with skin lichenoid changes on the forearms and dorsal hands (~15% BSA), mild oral mucosal lichenoid changes with xerostomia, and borderline dry eyes (Schirmer's 7 mm). No hepatic, pulmonary, GI, or musculoskeletal cGVHD. Tacrolimus taper was held; topical clobetasol initiated for skin, oral dexamethasone rinse for oral mucosa, and cyclosporine eye drops plus artificial tears initiated for ocular involvement. No systemic corticosteroids were required.

Today, on day +100, she comes in for her formal 100-day evaluation. Overall, she reports feeling progressively better. She is living at home with family support. She notes mild residual fatigue, which has improved significantly over the past 4 weeks. She rates her overall energy at 6/10 compared to 4/10 six weeks ago. The skin lichenoid changes on her forearms and hands are improving; she is applying clobetasol ointment twice daily as directed and notes decreased pruritus. Oral dryness and mild oral discomfort persist but are less bothersome -- she is using the dexamethasone rinse and notes improvement in the mucosal irritation. Dry eyes remain mildly symptomatic; she is using cyclosporine drops and artificial tears frequently. No dysphagia, odynophagia, or difficulty opening her mouth. No abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, or diarrhea. No evidence of GI GVHD symptomatology. No cough, dyspnea, or wheeze (no pulmonary GVHD symptoms). No jaundice, right upper quadrant pain, or weight change suggesting hepatic GVHD. No joint pain or stiffness. No muscle cramps or weakness.

She remains on tacrolimus, trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole (PCP prophylaxis; now off valganciclovir), acyclovir, and her outpatient medications including lisinopril and metformin. CMV PCR has been negative since day +60. She was successfully taken off valganciclovir as of today (day +100) per institutional protocol.

No recent hospitalizations. No new infections. No bleeding. Appetite improved; she has gained approximately 3 lbs over the past 4 weeks.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS:
Constitutional: Fatigue improving. No fever. No night sweats. Mild residual weight loss from induction/transplant period, now improving with weight gain.
Skin/mucous membranes: Improving lichenoid skin changes on forearms and dorsal hands, decreased pruritus. Mild xerostomia and buccal mucosal changes improving. No skin ulceration, blistering, or sclerosis.
Eyes: Mild dry eyes, managed with drops. No visual changes or blurring.
Oropharynx: Mild xerostomia. No dysphagia. No active oral ulcers.
Cardiovascular: No chest pain, palpitations, or edema.
Pulmonary: No cough, shortness of breath, or wheeze. No dyspnea on exertion beyond mild expected deconditioning.
Gastrointestinal: Normal bowel habits. No abdominal pain. No nausea or vomiting. No rectal bleeding or melena.
Genitourinary: No dysuria. Normal urine output. No hematuria.
Musculoskeletal: No joint swelling or pain. No muscle weakness.
Neurological: No headaches. No numbness or tingling. Fully resolved cerebellar symptoms from prior HiDAC neurotoxicity; gait normal. No focal neurologic deficits.
Hematologic/Infectious: No bleeding, bruising, or petechiae. No fever. No localizing signs of infection.
Endocrine/Metabolic: Blood sugars have been trending to improved control at home; continues metformin.
Psychiatric: Reports mild anxiety about upcoming disease restaging (day +180 bone marrow biopsy). Denies depression. Good family support system.

---

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY:
1. AML with myelodysplasia-related changes (AML-MRC), ELN 2022 adverse-risk -- see HPI above
2. Antecedent myelodysplastic syndrome (RCMD), diagnosed approximately 18 months prior to AML diagnosis, previously observed without treatment
3. Hypertension -- managed with lisinopril
4. Type 2 diabetes mellitus -- managed with metformin; tacrolimus-associated hyperglycemia monitored and managed
5. FLT3-ITD AML, status post 7+3 induction with midostaurin -- achieved MRD-negative CR1 after first induction
6. Status post HiDAC consolidation cycle 1 -- complicated by Grade 2 cerebellar neurotoxicity, fully resolved
7. Status post MUD PBSCT (10/10 match, RIC Flu/Bu2) -- day +100 today
8. Post-transplant CMV reactivation (day +45) -- treated and resolved
9. Mild chronic GVHD (skin, oral, ocular) -- on topical therapies, improving

SOCIAL HISTORY:
Ms. Okonkwo is a 48-year-old Nigerian-American woman who immigrated from Lagos approximately 20 years ago. She is a licensed practical nurse, currently on medical leave from work. She lives with family members who have been providing active support during her transplant recovery. Never-smoker. Rare alcohol use (none during treatment). No recreational drug use. She has expressed a strong desire to return to nursing work when medically cleared. Her transplant donor was a 10/10 MUD male donor.

FAMILY HISTORY:
No matched sibling donor was identified at the time of HLA typing. Specific family cancer history not extensively documented in chart; no known hereditary hematologic malignancies.

ALLERGIES:
No known drug allergies (NKDA).

MEDICATIONS:
1. Tacrolimus (Prograf) -- current dose being titrated per trough levels; trough today 8.2 ng/mL (target 8-12 ng/mL); taper to begin after day +100 evaluation if cGVHD stable
2. Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole (Bactrim SS) -- one tablet PO daily for PCP prophylaxis
3. Acyclovir 400 mg PO BID -- HSV/VZV prophylaxis
4. Valganciclovir 900 mg PO daily -- LAST DOSE TODAY; discontinuing per day +100 protocol (CMV PCR negative x 6+ weeks)
5. Prednisolone acetate eye drops -- ophthalmic GVHD management (in combination with artificial tears)
6. Cyclosporine 0.05% ophthalmic emulsion (Restasis) -- dry eyes/ocular GVHD
7. Clobetasol propionate 0.05% ointment -- topical, applied twice daily to affected skin areas (forearms, dorsal hands)
8. Dexamethasone oral rinse (0.5 mg/5 mL) -- swish and spit BID for oral mucosal GVHD
9. Lisinopril 10 mg PO daily -- hypertension
10. Metformin 1000 mg PO BID -- type 2 DM (resumed after transplant)
11. Pantoprazole 40 mg PO daily -- GI protection
12. Magnesium oxide 400 mg PO daily -- tacrolimus-associated hypomagnesemia supplementation
13. Artificial tears PRN -- ocular dryness
14. Pilocarpine 5 mg PO TID -- xerostomia management

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:
Vitals: Temperature 36.8 C, blood pressure 128/82 mmHg (stable on lisinopril), heart rate 78 bpm and regular, respiratory rate 16, oxygen saturation 97% on room air, weight 71.4 kg (BMI 26.1, up ~3 lbs from last visit).

General: Well-appearing, mildly fatigued-appearing woman in no acute distress. Alert and oriented x4. Conversational and engaged. Good affect.

Head/Eyes/Ears/Nose/Throat: Normocephalic, atraumatic. Sclera anicteric. Conjunctiva mildly injected bilaterally consistent with dry eye syndrome; no purulent discharge. Mild erythema of bulbar conjunctiva. Oropharynx: mild lichenoid changes of bilateral buccal mucosa and lateral tongue with slightly erythematous appearance; no active ulcerations; no white exudates; no trismus; mouth opening full. Mucous membranes mildly dry. No oral candidiasis. Lips intact.

Neck: Supple. No cervical lymphadenopathy. No thyromegaly or masses. JVP not elevated.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. Normal S1 and S2. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No peripheral edema.

Pulmonary: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No crackles, wheezing, or rhonchi. Good air movement throughout. No dullness to percussion.

Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. No hepatomegaly on palpation. Spleen not palpable (prior splenomegaly has resolved per recent imaging). No abdominal masses. Normal bowel sounds. No guarding or rigidity.

Skin: Lichenoid papular and planar eruption over bilateral forearms and dorsal hands, somewhat improved in extent and erythema compared to last visit (~10-12% BSA estimated today vs. ~15% BSA previously). No active weeping, blistering, ulceration, or sclerotic/indurated plaques. Residual mild post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation. No truncal or lower extremity skin GVHD. No rash on face, neck, or scalp. No petechiae or purpura.

Musculoskeletal: No joint swelling, warmth, or tenderness. Full range of motion in all major joints. No scleroderma-like skin tightening. Normal hand grip strength bilaterally.

Neurological: Alert and oriented. Cranial nerves II-XII intact. No dysmetria or ataxia on finger-nose-finger testing (fully resolved cerebellar toxicity from prior HiDAC). Gait steady and normal. Normal sensation to light touch in bilateral upper and lower extremities. No pronator drift.

Lymphatics: No palpable cervical, axillary, inguinal, or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS (TODAY):

Complete Blood Count:
- WBC: 4,800/uL (normal)
- ANC: 3,100/uL (adequate)
- Hemoglobin: 11.9 g/dL (mild anemia, improving; last visit 11.2 g/dL)
- MCV: 91 fL
- Platelets: 168,000/uL (normal)
- No circulating blasts on differential
- No atypical lymphocytes

Comprehensive Metabolic Panel:
- Sodium 138 mEq/L, potassium 3.8 mEq/L, chloride 102 mEq/L, bicarbonate 24 mEq/L
- BUN 14 mg/dL, creatinine 0.82 mg/dL (eGFR >60, stable renal function on tacrolimus)
- Glucose 108 mg/dL (fasting; acceptable on metformin)
- Total bilirubin 0.6 mg/dL (normal; no hepatic GVHD)
- ALT 22 U/L, AST 18 U/L (normal)
- Alkaline phosphatase 74 U/L (normal)
- Total protein 6.9 g/dL, albumin 3.8 g/dL
- Magnesium 1.8 mEq/L (low normal; on supplementation)

Tacrolimus trough: 8.2 ng/mL (within therapeutic range 8-12 ng/mL)

CMV PCR (blood): NEGATIVE (undetectable) -- eighth consecutive negative result; patient clearing from day +60

EBV PCR: NEGATIVE

HbA1c (obtained today): 6.8% -- type 2 DM well-controlled

---

IMAGING RESULTS:
No new imaging performed at today's visit. The most recent relevant imaging was the pre-transplant PET-CT (approximately 3.5 months ago at pre-transplant restaging), which showed no evidence of metabolically active disease, no extramedullary AML, and mild residual splenomegaly (12.1 cm, improved from 14.2 cm at diagnosis). Routine CT chest/abdomen/pelvis is planned as part of the 6-month (day +180) restaging evaluation, not today per protocol. No respiratory symptoms to prompt early chest imaging.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN:

Ms. Margaret Okonkwo is a 48-year-old woman with ELN adverse-risk AML-MRC (FLT3-ITD high allelic ratio, DNMT3A/ASXL1/SRSF2/RUNX1 co-mutations) who is now at day +100 following a 10/10 MUD PBSCT with RIC Flu/Bu2 conditioning. She achieved an excellent MRD-negative CR1 after first induction and has maintained full donor chimerism with molecular remission through her day +30 bone marrow evaluation. She is generally doing well at her 100-day milestone, with resolution of her early post-transplant complications and management of mild chronic GVHD.

1. AML-MRC, ELN adverse risk, in MRD-negative CR1, status post 10/10 MUD alloHSCT day +100:
   - The 100-day milestone is a critically important landmark in the post-transplant course, reflecting successful engraftment, early immune reconstitution, and early relapse risk assessment.
   - Per protocol, formal bone marrow biopsy and aspirate will be deferred to the day +180 evaluation. We will obtain chimerism testing by peripheral blood STR analysis today (whole blood, T-cell enriched fraction, and myeloid-enriched fraction) to confirm durable engraftment in the interim.
   - FLT3-ITD monitoring by peripheral blood quantitative PCR will also be sent as part of ongoing molecular surveillance.
   - DNMT3A p.R882H ddPCR will be sent for molecular remission confirmation.
   - CBC and metabolic panel reviewed and discussed above -- reassuringly, all within expected parameters for day +100. No cytopenias suggestive of early relapse or graft failure.
   - The peripheral blood differential shows no circulating blasts, which is reassuring.
   - She will be scheduled for comprehensive 6-month (day +180) restaging including bone marrow biopsy, CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast, and full chimerism and MRD panel.
   - Continue monitoring for signs of relapse: any new cytopenias, constitutional symptoms, bone pain, or extramedullary masses should prompt urgent evaluation.

2. Mild chronic GVHD (skin, oral, ocular) -- NIH Global Mild, improving:
   - Skin: Lichenoid changes continue to improve on topical clobetasol 0.05% ointment BID. Current extent estimated at approximately 10-12% BSA, down from ~15% at day +90. No signs of progression to sclerotic cGVHD. Continue topical clobetasol with plan to taper frequency once complete resolution achieved. Dermatology follow-up to be arranged if needed.
   - Oral: Lichenoid mucosal changes improving; xerostomia persistent but managed with oral dexamethasone rinse (swish and spit BID), pilocarpine 5 mg TID, and saliva substitutes. No active ulcers. Continue current regimen.
   - Ocular: Mild dry eye symptoms ongoing. Ophthalmology follow-up confirmed at 3-month intervals; Restasis (cyclosporine 0.05% ophthalmic emulsion) continued along with artificial tears. No corneal involvement.
   - No systemic immunosuppression beyond tacrolimus is required at this time, and the NIH Global Score remains mild (Score 1).
   - Given improvement in cGVHD manifestations, tacrolimus taper will be initiated after today's evaluation: reduce tacrolimus by approximately 0.5-1 mg every 4-6 weeks, with close monitoring of cGVHD manifestations and tacrolimus trough levels. Target: complete discontinuation by approximately month 12-15 post-transplant if cGVHD remains stable or improves. Patient counseled regarding importance of reporting any new or worsening GVHD symptoms promptly, particularly new GI symptoms (diarrhea, abdominal cramping, weight loss), new pulmonary symptoms (cough, dyspnea), or worsening skin/oral/ocular manifestations.

3. CMV management:
   - CMV PCR has been negative since day +60, with eight consecutive undetectable results.
   - Valganciclovir is being discontinued today at day +100 per standard institutional protocol. Patient counseled that CMV monitoring will continue with weekly PCR for the next 2 weeks after stopping, then every 2 weeks through day +180, with resumption of pre-emptive therapy if CMV reactivates above threshold (250 IU/mL).
   - Given D-/R+ serostatus mismatch, continued vigilance for late CMV reactivation is warranted, particularly as tacrolimus is being tapered and immune reconstitution evolves.

4. Infectious disease prophylaxis adjustments:
   - TMP-SMX (Bactrim) for PCP prophylaxis: continue per standard post-transplant protocol (typically through at least month 6-12 post-transplant, or until CD4 count >200/uL and off immunosuppression).
   - Acyclovir: continue for HSV/VZV prophylaxis; will transition to once-daily dosing at month 6 per protocol.
   - Antifungal prophylaxis: not currently on systemic antifungal agent. Will reassess if tacrolimus dose increases or if high-risk exposures arise.
   - Valganciclovir: discontinued today as above.

5. Immune reconstitution:
   - Lymphocyte subset panel to be ordered today. CD4 count and NK cell reconstitution data will guide adjustments to prophylaxis intensity and timing of vaccination program.
   - Inactivated vaccines should begin to be administered at approximately 6 months post-transplant per institutional protocol; will reassess at the day +180 visit.

6. Hypertension and diabetes:
   - Blood pressure 128/82 mmHg today -- well-controlled on lisinopril 10 mg daily. Continue as is. Note that ACE inhibitor continuation is appropriate for a patient with proteinuric risk on calcineurin inhibitor (tacrolimus). Monitor renal function.
   - HbA1c 6.8% -- excellent glycemic control on metformin. Continue metformin. Monitor blood glucose at home. Tacrolimus-associated hyperglycemia: blood glucose was more problematic in the early post-transplant period and has stabilized; as tacrolimus is tapered, glucose management should further improve.
   - Hypomagnesemia: serum magnesium 1.8 mEq/L, low normal -- continue magnesium supplementation. Tacrolimus is the likely cause; expect improvement as taper progresses.

7. Psychosocial and functional status:
   - ECOG performance status: 1 today.
   - Patient reports mild anxiety about upcoming restaging. Reassured that her clinical course to date has been excellent -- no circulating blasts, no cytopenias, engraftment confirmed, cGVHD mild and improving. Discussed the importance of the day +180 bone marrow biopsy as the definitive evaluation of remission status at 6 months.
   - Social work follow-up offered and appointment confirmed.
   - Patient currently on medical leave from nursing work. She expressed interest in returning to work. Advised that return to work, initially part-time or in a low-immunocompromised patient environment, may be considered at 12-18 months post-transplant pending immune reconstitution and stable clinical status. She will need to avoid high-infection-risk settings while on immunosuppression.

8. Late effects surveillance:
   - Given anthracycline exposure during induction and prior transplant conditioning, annual echocardiogram for cardiac late effects monitoring will be ordered at the 12-month visit.
   - Bone density (DEXA scan): schedule for 12 months post-transplant given corticosteroid use (topical clobetasol, low-grade systemic absorption) and post-transplant bone loss risk.
   - Secondary malignancy surveillance: routine cancer screening to be reinstated at 12-18 months per transplant late effects guidelines.
   - Pulmonary function tests: baseline post-transplant PFTs to be obtained at 6-month evaluation to screen for bronchiolitis obliterans syndrome (BOS) given cGVHD diagnosis.

FOLLOW-UP:
- Phone check-in with transplant NP in 2 weeks to review: CMV PCR status off valganciclovir, tacrolimus trough after dose reduction, any new GVHD symptoms.
- Next in-person clinic visit: 4 weeks (approximately day +130) for CBC, CMP, tacrolimus trough, CMV PCR, lymphocyte subsets review.
- Comprehensive 6-month (day +180) evaluation: scheduled; will include bone marrow biopsy/aspirate (MRD by 8-color flow, chimerism, FLT3-ITD/DNMT3A molecular testing), CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast, pulmonary function tests, and ophthalmology follow-up.

Total visit time: 55 minutes. Greater than 50% of visit time spent on counseling and coordination of care.

Electronically signed,
[BMT Attending Hematologist / Oncologist]
Hematology/Oncology and Bone Marrow Transplant Program
